coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the quenching mechanism of metal ions using solvent-driven N, S-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jingyan
Kalimuthu, Rajendran
Liu, Xingchen
Zhang, Wengao
Tan, Yonggen
Yan, Kun
Ye, Shenglin
Feng, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold great potential as fluorescence probes due to their tunable optical properties, but remain challenges in tailoring surface functionalization for selective metal ion detection, which is essential for environmental monitoring and water quality analysis. This work, synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) were via a one-pot solvothermal method with two different solvents, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetone. The influences of solvent selectivity on the surface functionality of CQDs and their fluorescence quenching mechanisms were evaluated in both experimental and computational methods. To represent CQDs surfaces, density functional theory (DFT) calculations of the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) were conducted. Our findings revealed that synthesized CQDs in DMF and acetone exhibited fluorescence quenching as static and dynamic. We have achieved the highest quantum yields of 37.85 % and 28.59 %. This shows excellent sensitivities of Fe3+ at 0.82 μM and 1.19 μM for Co2+. Furthermore, the study extended to validated in real water sample analysis. This approach enabled the development of a novel strategy for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
S-CQDs
Solvothermal
Iron (Fe),Cobalt (co) detection
Static
Dynamic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structural studies of N, S-CQDs
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N, S-CQDs
Influence of solvent on surface electron transfer properties
Time and pH stability of CQDs
Detection of heavy metal ions using N, S-CQDs
Selective and sensitivity of Fe3+/Co2+ ions
Computational study of CQDs with Fe3+/Co2+ ions
Detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in real water samples

This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No.2021M701589). We also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the one-step synthesis of N, S-CQDs, and its metal ion detection application.
(a) TEM images of 1-N, S-CQDs; (b) TEM images of 2-N, S-CQDs; (c) XRD patterns of 1-N, S-CQDs and 2-N, S-CQDs; (d) infrared spectra of 1-N, S-CQDs and 2-N, S-CQDs.
(a) Full survey XPS spectrum of 1-N, S-CQDs; fine spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, (f) S2p, (b) full survey XPS spectrum of 2-N, S-CQDs; fine spectra of (g) C1s, (h) N1s, (i) O1s, (j) S2p.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of 1-N, S-CQDs, (b) UV-VIS absorption spectrum, (c) Fluorescence quantum yields, (d) Fluorescence spectrum of 2-N, S-CQDs, (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (f) Fluorescence quantum yields.
Fluorescence spectrum of (a) 1-N, S-CQDs and (b) 2-N, S-CQDs for different metal ions; Intensity ratio of metal ion pairs (c) 1-N, S-CQDs and (d)2-N, S-CQDs after fluorescence quenching.

(a) The influence of different concentrations of Fe3+ on the fluorescence spectra of 1-N, S-CQDs and (b) the relationship between the concentration and the ratio of (F0–F)/F0; (c) The effect of different concentrations of Co2+ on the fluorescence spectra of 2-N, S-CQDs and (d) the relationship between the concentration and the ratio of (F0)–F/F0.
(a) UV absorption and (b) fluorescence lifetime comparison of 1-N, S-CQDs with and without Fe3+; (c) UV absorption and (d) Fluorescence lifetime of 2-N, S-CQDs with and without Co2+.
(a)TDOS of 1-N,S-CQDs/Fe3+ (b)PDOS of Fe atom (c)PDOS of N atoms; (d)TDOS of 2-N,S-CQDs/Co2+ (e)PDOS of Co atom (f)PDOS of O atoms; The vertical dotted line corresponds to the HOMO level.
Schematic diagram illustrating fluorescence quenching based on the energy levels of (a)1-N,S-CQDs, Fe3+ ions, (b)2-N,S-CQDs, Co2+ ions and the photoelectron transfer process.
Schematic diagram of photophysical processes and internal charge transfer (ICT) processes of N,S-CQDs-Fe3+/Co2+.
Detection results of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Unveiling the quenching mechanism of metal ions using solvent-driven N, S-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen, 518055, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, Guangdong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold great potential as fluorescence probes due to their tunable optical properties, but remain challenges in tailoring surface functionalization for selective metal ion detection, which is essential for environmental monitoring and water quality analysis. This work, synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) were via a one-pot solvothermal method with two different solvents, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetone. The influences of solvent selectivity on the surface functionality of CQDs and their fluorescence quenching mechanisms were evaluated in both experimental and computational methods. To represent CQDs surfaces, density functional theory (DFT) calculations of the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) were conducted. Our findings revealed that synthesized CQDs in DMF and acetone exhibited fluorescence quenching as static and dynamic. We have achieved the highest quantum yields of 37.85 % and 28.59 %. This shows excellent sensitivities of Fe3+ at 0.82 μM and 1.19 μM for Co2+. Furthermore, the study extended to validated in real water sample analysis. This approach enabled the development of a novel strategy for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions.

Scheme 1 Schematic representation of the one-step synthesis of N, S-CQDs, and its metal ion detection application.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past century, the industry worldwide has rapidly expanded and the need for new materials has increased tremendously. As a result, it has raised the demand for the reckless exploitation of Earth's natural resources, worsening global environmental pollution. Heavy metal contamination is becoming a global issue due to its negative impacts. The environment is polluted by metal ions, particularly iron (Fe) and cobalt (Co), Which has garnered increasing attention [1,2]. Iron is essential for humans and animals, vital in hemoglobin synthesis and oxygen transport. However, imbalances of iron levels whether excessive or deficient can lead to health issues such as anemia and neurological damage [3,4] Similarly, cobalt ions (Co2+) are crucial for enzyme catalysis and red blood cell production, but excessive cobalt exposure can damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system [5]. When industrial waste containing these metal ions being discharged into water bodies without adequate treatment, these metal ions contribute to water pollution and disrupt the aquatic ecosystem. Therefore, monitoring the concentrations of Fe3+ and Co2+ in water has become a critical focus in environmental monitoring. Traditional metal ion detection techniques, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [6], ion chromatography [7], and colorimetry [8], are accurate but often hindered by limitations such as costly equipment, complex procedures, and time-consuming sample preparation, making them less suitable for large-scale monitoring. In contrast, fluorescence detection offers high sensitivity, ease of use, and cost-effectiveness, making it an attractive alternative for metal ion analysis. The development of fluorescence sensors not only enhances detection efficiency but also enables rapid, real-time environmental monitoring [9–12].

Among various fluorescent sensors and traditional semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as an ideal choice for metal ion detection and practical applications due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, simple synthetic methods and good biocompatibility. As zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, CQDs possess surface-functional groups that enable strong interactions with metal ions, making them highly sensitive probes for ion detection. [13,14]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield and photoluminescent performance of CQDs are often hindered by surface defects and structural characteristics. As a result, improving the fluorescence performance of CQDs has become a central research focus. Surface modification, especially passivation treatment with different dopants such as cysteine and thiourea, effectively rectifies these defects [11], boosting both the fluorescence quantum yield and the affinity for metal ions [15]. These surface modifications enhance the optical properties of CQDs and significantly improve their selectivity and sensitivity to metal ions, such as Fe3+ and Co2+. Heteroatom doping has been shown to be effective in tuning fluorescence emission, increasing the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQYs) of CQDs, and even modifying their optical properties. The higher electron affinity and electronegativity of nitrogen enhance the chemical reactivity of CQDs, while the moderate electron affinity and ionization energy of sulfur facilitate controlled doping within CQDs. Co-doping nitrogen and sulfur into CQDs modifies their energy level and structure, enhances fluorescence intensity, and adjusts the emission peak position and tunable wavelength [16–18], thereby enhancing their application in fluorescence probes and analytical detection. Annamalai et al. [19] synthesized N, S-CQDs using cysteine and ethylenediamine, which exhibited excellent quenching responses toward picric acid (PA) and Fe3+ ions, demonstrating highly sensitive detection limits. The linear detection ranges for Fe3+ and PA were 0–50 μM, with detection limits (LODs) of 0.11 μM and 0.07 μM, respectively. Gao et al. [20] prepared N, S-CQDs with a high quantum yield of 77.68 % by using Tetrasodium Dicarboxymethyl Aspartate and m-phenylenediamine. These CQDs exhibited excellent optical properties and functioned as “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for detecting Hg2+ and MnO4−, with detection limits of 0.41 μM and 1.2 μM, respectively.

However, the existing fluorescence-based sensors for detection of Fe and Co ions have some limitations, such as low selectivity, complex synthesis procedures, toxic reagents, stability, reproducibility, and application in real samples. To overcome these drawbacks, it's indispensable to develop the dope or modify CQDs sensors that have emerged in the environmental detection of metal ions.

The present work offers simple one-spot synthesis, good stability in aqueous medium, high sensitivity and selectivity, excellent fluorescence, and good accuracy and reproducibility. The synthesis of doped CQDs with citric acid and thiourea as precursors, yields two types of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs with unique surface properties and high quantum yield for the detection of metal ions. The suitable geometrical models for the different surface groups of CQDs are shown in (S1). Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of CQDs efficient fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ and Co2+ ions.

All reagents and chemicals used in this study were purchased and used without further purification. Citric acid, thiourea, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and acetone were obtained from Aladdin. The ionic solutions were prepared using the following purchased materials: Fe(NO3)3, CrCl3, ZnCl2, and NiSO4 from Yuanye Bio; CuSO4, MnSO4, CoSO4, and NaCl from McLin. The experiment used double-deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) produced by the laboratory's ultrapure water system (PF-10D).

2 mmol of citric acid and 6 mmol of thiourea were added as carbon and sulfur sources of precursors, mixing with solvent using either 20 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), or acetone. Furthermore, the solution was sonicated for 30 min until completely dissolved. The resultant mixture was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave lined with poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon) and heated to 180 °C for 8 h. The reaction mixture was centrifuged and then purified for three days using dialysis (3500 Da) [21]. The resultant N, S-CQDs were freeze-dried to yield a powder for further investigation. The doping ratio of citric acid and thiourea was estimated for molar ratios of 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4 mmol by varying the carbon and sulfur contents. The different ratio of CQDs fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. S2. Based on the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield, a 1:3 ratio was fixed for future studies.

The Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) morphology was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2F30, Czech Republic), with high-resolution TEM employed for lattice structure analysis. The elemental composition and functional groups were identified using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Vertex 70v, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Smartlab 3 KW, Japan) was performed to analyze the diffraction peaks of the CQDs in the 10°–80° 2θ range (scan rate: 10°/min, step size: 0.01°/s). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence properties, including emission spectra, fluorescence lifetime decay curves, and absolute quantum yield, were measured using an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher ESCALAB Xi+, USA) was used for surface chemical state analysis, with the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV employed for spectral calibration. The Dmol3 algorithm is used in DFT computations, which use numerical functions on an atom-centered grid as an atomic basis to determine the geometric optimization and properties of CQDs [22,23]. The exchange-correlation function is the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) approach [24]. The van der Waals (vdW) interaction is described using the DFT-D approach in the Grimme scheme [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural studies of N, S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The HR TEM analysis N, S-CQDs as shown in Fig. 2 (a&b), suggested the lattice spacing of 1-N, S-CQDs, and 2-N, S-CQDs, to be 0.318 nm and 0.337 nm, respectively, based on Fourier transform analysis of selected areas. These values are close to the ideal graphite interlayer spacing of 0.335 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane. They all exhibit similar spherical shapes, with particle sizes ranging from 4 to 9 nm, indicating good crystallinity. This result is consistent with numerous previous reports [19,26]. Fig. 2(c) presents the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of N, S-CQDs, where 1-N, S-CQDs exhibit a distinct peak at 2θ = 27.3°, and 2-N, S-CQDs shows a peak at 24.8°. Using the Bragg equation, the corresponding lattice spacing was calculated to be 0.325 nm and 0.348 nm, respectively, corresponding to the graphite plane (002). Moreover, the acidic nature of citric acid in the precursor, combined with the alkaline conditions provided by DMF, promotes a dehydration reaction before the carbonization process. This accelerated dehydration enhances the carbonization of citric acid [27], reducing defects and functional groups within the carbon core of the final product.

In contrast, while acetone is used as the solvent, surface and defect states are introduced into the carbon core during the formation process, resulting in a higher degree of carbonization occurring in the 1-N, S-CQDs compared to 2-N, S-CQDs. These findings are consistent with the observed shift of the X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks to higher angles.

To further investigate the surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N, S-CQDs, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on both synthesized CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(d). A broad peak found at 3440 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H functional groups, while the absorption band at 3200 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration. A sharp and intense peak was observed at 2220 cm−1, which arose from the stretching vibration of S–C ≡N [28]. The peak at 1730 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C=O, and the absorption peak at 1574 cm−1 correlates to the bending vibrations of C=C/C=N. Additionally, the absorption band near 550 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–S/S–S bonds. Notably, the characteristic absorption peak of the S=O bond is observed only in the 2-N, S-CQDs at 1200 cm−1 [29]. A detailed analysis of the FTIR spectra reveals that both types of CQDs contain more surface functional groups, including –NH2, –COOH, –OH, and sulfur-containing groups such as –SCN, C–S, and S–S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N, S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is an important analysis for identifying the surface composition of the structure in CQDs. By examining their chemical bonds and states, XPS provides a clear understanding of the CQDs' structure. As shown in Fig. 3 (a&b), the full survey spectra exhibit four peaks corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p. Specifically, in Fig. 3(a), the elemental contents of 1-N, S-CQDs are 20.11 %, 20.21 %, 55.89 %, and 3.79 %, respectively, while in Fig. 3(b), the elemental contents of 2-N, S-CQDs are 50.85 %, 9.3 %, 32.85 %, and 7.47 %. Fig. 3(c–f) shows the high-resolution spectra for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p of 1-N, S-CQDs. After peak fitting the C 1s spectrum, three splitting peaks were identified at 283.9 eV, 284.8 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C–S, C–C/C=C, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [30]. After fitting the N1s spectrum, two peaks were observed at 399.1 eV and 396.8 eV, corresponding to amino nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively [31]. The O 1s spectrum revealed two characteristic peaks at 530.7 eV and 532.3 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–O–C/C–OH bonds [32]. After fitting the S2p spectrum, four peaks were observed at 161.6 eV, 162.8 eV, 163.4 eV, and 164.6 eV, corresponding to C–S, C–S–C, S–C ≡N, and S–S bonds, respectively [33].

Fig. 3(g–j) shows the high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p for 2-N, S-CQDs. Compared to 1-N, S-CQDs, the full spectrum of 2-N, S-CQDs Fig. 3(b) exhibits a different elemental composition. The O 1s content significantly increased to 32.38 %, and as well as sulfur content also increased. The increase in sulfur is associated with the appearance of C-SOx groups on the surface of the CQDs, as shown by the additional peaks at 167.5 eV and 168.7 eV in Fig. 3(j), which correspond to C–SO2 and C–SO3 bonds [34]. The N 1s content significantly decreased from 20.21 % to 9.3 %, as acetone, unlike DMF, does not contain nitrogen, resulting in reduced nitrogen doping during the reaction. The XPS analysis shows that the synthesized CQDs have a rich carbon content and the addition of thiourea effectively incorporated nitrogen and sulfur content into the CQDs. Moreover, these functional groups on the surface induced excellent solubility in aqueous systems without any further chemical modification of the CQDs. This will make them suitable for environmental monitoring applications [35].

Optical properties of as-synthesized N, S-CQDs were conducted using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 4 (a&b) Fluorescence emission spectra were measured with excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 480 nm. The result reveals that the emission peak position and intensity depend on the excitation wavelength, a characteristic commonly observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials. This behavior is likely attributed to the distribution of different emission sites on each nanoparticle [36]. As shown in Fig. 4(a), when excited at 360 nm, the 1-N, S-CQDs exhibit an emission peak maximum at 450 nm. While increasing the excitation wavelength to 460 nm, the emission peak red shifted at 568 nm, and the FWHM (full width at half maximum) of 118 nm. Fig. 4(d) presents the fluorescence spectra of 2-N, S-CQDs. As the excitation wavelength increases from 360 nm to 460 nm, the emission peak shifts from 470 nm to 528 nm, exhibiting a redshift of 58 nm. The nitrogen and sulfur doping introduces defect states on the surface of the CQDs, thereby enhancing their oxidation state.

The alterations previously described are likely the principal reasons for the observed ultra-broad redshift in fluorescence emission [16].Fig. 4(b&e) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of 1-N, S-CQDs and 2-N, S-CQDs, respectively, the absorption peaks observed at 347 nm and 360 nm, which are attributed to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ electronic transitions of C=N, C=O, and –NH2 surface functional groups on the CQDs [37]. Additionally, absorption peaks are observed at longer wavelengths 478 nm and 463 nm, which can be attributed to the new energy level transition introduced by the doped nitrogen and sulfur. These functional groups S–C ≡N, S–S, and –NH2 modify the surface chemical environment of the CQDs, leading to new absorption bands and defect states. These absorption peaks are typically broader and concentrated in the wavelength range of 400–550 nm [38].

Furthermore, the quantum yields of 1-N, S-CQDs, and 2-N, S-CQDs were measured using an integrating sphere coupled with a fluorescence spectrometer. The sample concentration was 0.05 mg/mL, with an absorbance of approximately 0.05. The quantum yields were calculated, and shown in Fig. 4 (c&f), Which 1-N, S-CQDs is 37.85 %, and 2-N, S-CQDs is 28.59 % respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of solvent on surface electron transfer properties
ce:section.ce:para: The solvent environment has a considerable impact on CQD emission spectrum. DMF, a nitrogen-rich alkaline solvent, contains amino groups that facilitate the dehydration of the acidic nature of citric acid during the reaction. As a result, CQDs synthesized in DMF have more complete carbon core structures, promoting the formation of more stable carbon quantum dots and increasing quantum yield [39,40]. Acetone's highly electrophilic carbonyl group facilitates interactions with nucleophiles, such as thiourea, enabling the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into CQDs. This increases the sulfur doping level, resulting in the production of additional sulfur-containing groups.

Furthermore, the high polarity of DMF facilitates the formation of complex defects in CQD surfaces. Defect states frequently lead to changes in the energy level structure, which influence the wavelength and intensity of fluorescence emission [38,41]. As the excitation wavelength increases, surface and defect states become more readily excited, shifting the emission peak to longer wavelengths [34]. In contrast, Acetone's low polarity causes more surface defects, which increases non-radiative transitions and reduces quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time and pH stability of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CQDs is a critical factor influencing their optical performance in sensing applications. In this study, we evaluated synthetic CQDs temporal and pH stability. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the fluorescence intensity of 1-N, S-CQDs exhibits minimal variation across different pH values despite slight shifts in the peak position. Similarly, Fig. S3(c) demonstrates the temporal stability of the fluorescence spectrum for 2-N, S-CQDs, which appears more stable than that of 1-N, S-CQDs. Fig. S3(b&d) further highlight the pH stability of the respective CQDs. Additionally, as shown in Figs. S4(a–d), the time stability of the CQDs fluorescence remained unchanged over 90 days. These findings suggest that the synthesized CQDs exhibited excellent stability in both pH and time, making them highly suitable fluorescent probes for sensing applications without requiring special treatment for environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy metal ions using N, S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized CQDs were utilized as a fluorescence probe to measure the sensitivity of different metal ions under ideal conditions. A fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence intensity of CQDs interacting with different metal ions, and Fig. 5
(a&b) illustrates how the intensity changes over time. Of the metal ions that were tested (Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, Fe3+, Ni2+, and Co2+), only the Fe3+ and Co2+ ions showed significant spectrum changes. This confirms that, without any modification, the synthesized CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ and Co2+ ions in aqueous medium. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded at a fixed excitation wavelength at 380 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity denoted as F, and F0 representing the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs without metal ions. The normalized fluorescence intensity ratio is presented in Fig. 5 (c&d). Specifically, 1-N, S-CQDs demonstrated unique selectivity for Fe3+, whereas 2-N, S-CQDs exhibited the strongest quenching effect with Co2+. To further investigate the effects of Fe3+ and Co2+, solutions with varying concentrations of these ions were prepared to investigate their quenching effects on N, S-CQDs solutions. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching detection limits (LOD) and the underlying mechanism of quenching were studied.

Considering the interference of other metal ions on Fe3+ and Co2+ during the detection process, we conducted an anti-interference experiment to evaluate the impact of Fe3+ and Co2+ in the presence of various metal ions. As shown in Fig. S5(a&b), when 100 μM of the interfering ions are present in the CQDs solutions, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs solution remains virtually unchanged. However, when equal concentrations of Fe3+ (100 μM) and Co2+ (100 μM) are added, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs solution still shows a noticeable quenching effect. These results suggest that the interference from co-existing metal ions has a negligible impact on the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitivity of Fe3+/Co2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) demonstrates the effect of Fe3+ concentration (ranging from 0 to 160 μM) on the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the 1-N, S-CQD solution. While increasing the concentration of Fe3+ the PL intensity of the solution decreases progressively, accompanied by a red shift in the fluorescence emission. Fig. 6(c) illustrates the correlation between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity. The plot shows the linear relationship of (F0 - F)/F0 versus Fe3+ concentration, with the linear regression equation given by y = 0.0172x + 0.175 (R2 = 0.991). The detection limit 0–160 μM, with the standard deviation (σ) of blank control samples being 0.0047. Based on these results, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 0.82 μM using equation (3). This value is consistent with the European Union (EU) regulation on the maximum allowable concentration of Fe3+ ions in drinking water, which is set at 3.57 μM [42]. Compared with the previous reports, the obtained N, S-CQDs showed higher detection limit (0.82 μM), as summarized in Table S1. This method offers a superior detection range and sensitivity, with a lower detection limit than existing sensors, and surpasses international regulatory standards. For real-time tests of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions in water samples. Rapid fluorescence response of CQDs and the strong fluorescence quenching behavior of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions can be used as a fluorescent probe. Changing the concentration of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions in the CQDs solution, the quantitative analysis of these ions was completed.

Fig. 6(b&d) shows the effects of Co2+ at varying concentrations on the PL spectra of 2-N, S-CQDs, along with the linear relationship between concentration and the (F0 - F)/F0 ratio. The detection limit is from 0 to 160 μM, with the linear regression coefficient (R2 = 0.985) achieved by this equation given by y = 0.0108x + 0.0740. The standard deviation (σ) for the blank control group is 0.0043, resulting in a detection limit (LOD) of 1.19 μM. This LOD is significantly lower than the maximum permissible concentration of cobalt in workplace air (50 μM) as recommended by the EU Air Quality Directive [43]. Furthermore, compared to a recent literature survey, this method offers significant advantages in both detection limit and sensitivity. The achieved detection limit is notably lower (as shown in Table S2), making this approach highly suitable for meeting international detection standards.

Fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to dynamic quenching (DQE) or static quenching (SQE) [44], both of which follow the traditional Stern-Volmer equation. SQE occurs due to a strong interaction between the fluorescent probe and the quenchers, resulting in the formation of non-fluorescent complexes in the ground state, which leads to a quenching of the fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs remains unaffected, as evidenced by the appearance of new absorption peaks in the UV–visible absorption spectrum. In contrast, DQE involves non-radiative transitions, where excited-state electrons in CQDs collide with metal ions, returning to the ground state [45], during which their fluorescence lifetime is significantly shortened but the UV absorption spectrum does not change.

To better understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism induced by Fe3+ and Co2+, we have investigated changes in the UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) decay of N, S-CQDs. Fig. 7 (a) shows a notable shift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum upon the addition of an appropriate amount of Fe3+ to the 1-N, S-CQD solution. Fig. 7(b) presents the photoluminescence (PL) decay data, measured using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) and fitted with a biexponential function. The average PL lifetime of 1-N, S-CQDs was calculated at 10.66 ns. With the further addition of Fe3+, the lifetime decreased slightly to 10.59 ns, indicating that the fluorescence lifetime of 1-N, S-CQDs remains unchanged before and after the addition of Fe3+. This suggests that there is no substantial excited-state interaction between 1-N, S-CQDs, and Fe3+ ions on the excited state.

Based on these observations, we found that the fluorescence quenching induced by Fe3+ ions resulting the formation of a stable non-fluorescent complex between the excited-state surface electrons with 1-N, S-CQDs, and the ground-state Fe3+, leading to static quenching [46,47]. In contrast, for 2-N, S-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 7(c&d), the UV absorption peak remains almost unchanged after the addition of Co2+. However, the fluorescence lifetime decreases from 11.16 ns to 10.42 ns. There is no non-fluorescent complex formed during the interaction between the 2-N, S-CQDs, and Co2+ ions.

The recent reports also confirm that the presence of sulfur-containing groups (e.g., –SCN, -C-SOx-, –SH, etc.) can enhance the interaction between Co2+ and N, S-CQDs, facilitating energy transfer from the excited-state electrons on the surface of CQDs to the quencher, rather than returning to the ground state via photon emission [48], resulting in non-radiative transitions. Therefore, we conclude that the fluorescence quenching of Co2+ions by the 2-N, S-CQDs is dynamic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational study of CQDs with Fe3+/Co2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between CQDs and Fe3+/Co2+ was investigated by analyzing the density of states (DOS) of 1-N,S-CQDs/Fe3+ and 2-N,S-CQDs/Co2+ (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 8(a) shows the total density of states (TDOS) of the α and β spins for 1-N, S-CQDs/Fe3+ are almost identical. Fig. 8(b&c) depicts how the partial density of states (PDOS) of the two atoms overlaps with the PDOS spectra, the interaction between the 3d orbitals of Fe and the 2p orbitals of N atoms. This overlapping demonstrates the formation of coordination links between Fe and the –NH2 group. Similarly, Fig. 8(d–f) shows the same DOS of the α and β spins for 2-N, S-CQDs/Co2+. According to the PDOS spectra, coordination bonds are formed between Co and the 2p orbitals of O, due to interactions between the 3d orbitals of Co and the –SO3H group. These results confirm that N, S-CQDs form coordination bonds with Fe3+ and Co2+ ions.

The findings show that by favoring the formation of electrostatic attraction with metal ions, the valence band maximum (VBM) sites of hexagonal-shaped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), such as those functionalized with amine and sulfur groups, are appropriate for metal ion detection. The band edge positions are greatly influenced by the functional group placement, which enables the tunability of CQD features in a range of sensing applications. For instance, 1-N,S-CQDs have a VBM of −5.41 eV, whereas 2-N,S-CQDs have a VBM of −5.05 eV.The electronic characteristics of the excited states were analyzed using molecular orbital theory. Fluorescence emission predominantly occurs from the lowest excited state, as described by Kasha's rule [49].

Only the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were thus taken into account. Fig. 9 displays the electronic configuration of the complex and CQDs, respectively, in addition to the outermost molecular orbitals. With matching energy gaps of 1.33 eV and 1.62 eV, the LUMO levels of 1-N,S-CQDs and 2-N,S-CQDs are at −4.08 eV and −3.43 eV, respectively. By establishing non-radiative decay pathways, the d-orbital values of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions (−4.11 eV, −5.36 eV for Fe3+; −3.42 eV, −5.03 eV for Co2+) enable fluorescence quenching when they interact with CQDs. The fluorescence behavior is affected by these interactions, which move the HOMO electron density from CQDs to the metal ions. The creation of hydrogen bonds, which affects the charge distribution in excited states and is essential to the quenching mechanism, is responsible for the interaction.

The photophysical process depicted in Fig. 10 demonstrates that the molecule is initially excited from the ground state (S0) to the first excited state (S1). From S1, the molecule can return to S0 via two main pathways: radiative decay (KF) and nonradiative internal conversion (KIC). In the S1 excited state of N,S-CQDs, electrons from LUMO are transferred to the partially filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ and Co2+ ions. This electron transfer from N,S-CQDs to the metal ions interferes with the recombination of electron-hole pairs within the N,S-CQDs, resulting in a reduction in fluorescence intensity, or fluorescence quenching [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and Co2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection method proposed in this study was validated using real water samples, with the results summarized in Table 1 .

The recovery rates of Fe3+ detected by 1-N, S-CQDs in lake water and tap water ranged from 91.95 % to 106.64 % and 93.39 %–105.46 %, respectively, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 0.68 %–1.14 % and 1.60 %–1.98 %. Similarly, for the detection of Co2+ using 2-N, S-CQDs, the recovery rates in lake water and tap water ranged from 93.47 % to 106.6 % and 97.02 %–100.01 %, with RSD of 0.89 %–2.02 % and 0.25 %–1.27 %, respectively. These findings strongly support the application of 1-N, S-CQDs/Fe3+ and 2-N, S-CQDs/Co2+ fluorescent probes in real water samples, demonstrating the high efficiency and reliability of the proposed method.

This study synthesizes nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) using citric acid and thiourea as precursors, with DMF and acetone used separately as solvents. The fluorescence quenching mechanism strategy for selective Fe3+ and Co2+ detection is verified. The highest quantum yield was obtained with DMF (37.85 %) and acetone (28.59 %), with amine and sulfur functionalization playing a key role in the detection. The surfaces of CQDs were modeled using density functional theory (DFT), providing insights for future applications in metal ion detection.

Jingyan Yu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rajendran Kalimuthu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xingchen Liu: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Wengao Zhang: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yonggen Tan: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Kun Yan: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shenglin Ye: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Jun Feng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Southern University of Science and Technology and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory Program under contract No.2021B1212040001. This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No.2021M701589). We also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of nitroaromatic explosives using π-electron rich luminescent polymeric nanocomposites
RSC Adv.
